title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Aura in the Age of AI Art
content.subtitle: Questioning the nature of creativity as machines begin to master the canvas.
content.text: In 1935, the philosopher Walter Benjamin argued that the 'aura' of a work of art—its unique existence in time and space—was being withered by mechanical reproduction, such as photography and film. Today, we face a new crisis of authenticity. [31] With the rise of generative artificial intelligence, the act of creation has been decoupled from the human hand, raising the question of whether a machine-made image can ever possess a 'soul.' [/31] If art is a reflection of the human condition, what does it mean when the 'artist' is a set of statistical probabilities? || The process of AI art generation is often misunderstood. It is not a simple 'copy-paste' from the internet; rather, the AI is trained on billions of images, learning the mathematical relationships between pixels, textures, and styles. [32] When a user inputs a prompt, the AI isn't searching a database, but is instead 'hallucinating' a new image based on these learned patterns. [/32] This has led to a democratization of creativity, allowing those without technical skills to bring their visions to life. Yet, for many professional illustrators, this is seen as an act of 'algorithmic plagiarism' that devalues years of dedicated practice. || [33] The debate often centers on the definition of 'intent.' Traditionalists argue that art requires a conscious mind that makes deliberate choices based on emotion and experience. [/33] In contrast, an AI has no feelings, no history, and no motivation. It doesn't 'choose' a color because it feels sad; it chooses it because the prompt and the training data suggest it is the most likely fit. However, proponents of AI art argue that the 'intent' has simply shifted from the brushstroke to the prompt itself—the artist is now a director rather than a performer. || [34] This technological shift is also disrupting the art market. In 2018, an AI-generated portrait sold at a major auction house for over $400,000, shocking the world and signaling that collectors are willing to invest in non-human creativity. [/34] This has sparked a legal and ethical battleground over copyright. If an AI is trained on the work of living artists without their consent, who owns the resulting image? The coder? The user who wrote the prompt? Or the machine itself? Currently, the law is struggling to keep pace with the speed of innovation. || [35] Some artists have responded by embracing 'glitch' and 'lo-fi' aesthetics, intentionally creating works that highlight the imperfections and 'uncanny valley' effects of early AI. [/35] By leaning into the bizarre and the broken, they are attempting to reclaim a space that the machine cannot yet perfectly replicate. They are finding beauty in the errors, a quintessentially human trait that values the 'flaw' as a mark of authenticity. || [36] Looking forward, it is unlikely that AI will replace the human artist entirely. [/36] Instead, we are likely to see a synthesis where AI becomes another tool in the creative kit, much like the camera or the synthesizer once were. The value of art may shift away from technical execution toward the 'concept' and the 'narrative'—the story that only a human, with a lifetime of lived experience, can truly tell. The machine can provide the image, but the human must provide the meaning.

questions.question: What 'crisis' does the author identify in the first paragraph?
options: A) The lack of interest in traditional art among younger generations.
B) The difficulty of preserving machine-made images in physical spaces.
C) The separation of the creative process from human physical effort.
D) The decline of photography as a respected art form.
questions.explanation: The text states that the 'act of creation has been decoupled from the human hand,' which is the new crisis of authenticity.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, how does AI generate an image?
options: A) By combining several existing images from its database into one.
B) By performing a complex search for the most relevant internet photos.
C) By creating an original work based on mathematical patterns it has studied.
D) By following a set of rules provided by professional illustrators.
questions.explanation: The author explains it is not 'copy-paste' but 'hallucinating' new images based on 'mathematical relationships' and 'learned patterns.'

questions.question: What is the 'traditionalist' objection to AI-generated art?
options: A) It is too expensive for the average collector to buy.
B) It lacks the emotional depth that comes from conscious decision-making.
C) It relies too heavily on sad colors and themes.
D) It is impossible to use as a tool for teaching technical skills.
questions.explanation: They argue art requires a 'conscious mind' making 'deliberate choices based on emotion,' which the AI lacks.

questions.question: What was significant about the auction house sale in 2018?
options: A) It proved that AI-generated art is legally protected by copyright.
B) It showed that the market is beginning to value non-human art financially.
C) It was the first time an artist was sued for using AI technology.
D) It resulted in a new law that identifies machines as legal owners of art.
questions.explanation: The sale 'shocked the world' and signaled that 'collectors are willing to invest in non-human creativity.'

questions.question: Why are some artists using 'glitch' and 'lo-fi' styles?
options: A) Because they are unable to use AI tools effectively.
B) To make their work more appealing to digital collectors.
C) To emphasize human-like imperfections that AI struggles to mimic.
D) Because they want to help AI machines improve their accuracy.
questions.explanation: They are 'reclaiming a space' that the machine can't replicate by valuing the 'flaw' as a mark of authenticity.

questions.question: In the conclusion, the author predicts that in the future...
options: A) technical skill will be the most important part of an artist's value.
B) humans will focus on the meaning and narrative behind the art.
C) AI will eventually develop its own lived experiences and stories.
D) cameras and synthesizers will be replaced by AI tools.
questions.explanation: The author suggests value will shift toward 'concept' and 'narrative' because 'human must provide the meaning.'
